extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A thermal incubator system comprising an insulated box with a heatbed, temperature sensors (T2, T3), a fan, and a software system for communication, control, and simulation. Key components include: 1) Heatbed with thermal inertia, 2) Insulation to minimize heat loss, 3) Fan for air circulation (operated at 12V/18V in experiments), 4) Controller for temperature regulation, 5) Simulation models (Model A: 2 parameters; Model B: 4 parameters) for predicting thermal behavior. Operational domain: Maintaining stable temperatures for biological/medical applications. Key characteristics: Thermal inertia prediction, temperature uniformity testing, and calibration for accurate model parameters (e.g., C_air=616.56 J/K, G_box=0.65 J/K for Model A).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: 1) Real-time visualization of incubator status via dashboard using controller and driver data. 2) On-demand calibration of simulation models (Model B) using Kalman filter for heatbed state estimation. 3) Anomaly detection through Kalman filter correlation with non-measured heatbed temperature. 4) What-if simulations for controller optimization using co-simulations with historical/real-time data. 5) Self-adaptation loop: Detects plant behavior changes, schedules experiments, reconfigures controllers, and updates models (e.g., recalibrates Model B parameters after new experiments). Target users: Engineers, researchers, and system operators requiring predictive analytics and control optimization.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: 1) RabbitMQ (open-source message broker) for communication between controller, low-level driver, and Digital Twin. 2) Low-level driver for sensor/actuator interaction with safety protocols and periodic temperature data upload. 3) Simulation models (Model A/B) with calibrated parameters (e.g., C_air, G_box, C_heater, G_heater). 4) Time series database for storing controller messages. 5) Kalman filter for state estimation and anomaly detection. 6) Open-source hardware/software platform (GitHub repository) with accessible datasets. 7) Co-simulation framework for what-if scenarios using historical/real-time data from Physical Twin.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time synchronization between physical and digital components via RabbitMQ communication server, with Kalman filter state estimation updates and what-if simulations using historical/real-time data. Temporal granularity ensures rapid response to anomalies (e.g., 2-second air circulation cycle for fan).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Multiple digital twin instances (Model A/B with 2/4 parameters) and hierarchical structure: centralized communication server (RabbitMQ) coordinates decentralized components (low-level driver, controller). Three temperature sensors enable multipoint monitoring, with Model B handling heatbed inertia prediction.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestrated architecture integrating RabbitMQ as message broker, low-level driver for PT state reporting, controller with bang-bang logic, and simulation models (Model A/B). Calibration algorithms synchronize physical and digital states, with Kalman filter correlating sensor data to non-measured heatbed parameters.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Open-source hardware/software integration via GitHub repository. Uses RabbitMQ (AMQP protocol) for interoperability with external systems, enabling data exchange with monitoring tools and co-simulation platforms. Supports enterprise integration through standardized communication protocols.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin models include behavioral (differential equations for dynamic analysis), analytical (simulation models for thermal inertia prediction), and geometric (CAD models for physical structure). Data sources are sensors on the Physical Twin (PT) collecting operational data over time. Models are calibrated using algorithms (e.g., Kalman filter) to update parameters like C_air (616.56 J/K) and G_box (0.65 J/K for Model A; 486.12 J/K, 0.856 J/K, 33.65 J/K, 0.87 J/K for Model B). Data management involves real-time synchronization via RabbitMQ, with models integrated into a Digital Twin framework for simulation, monitoring, and optimization.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators include a fan (12V/18V variable voltage) and heatbed. Control mechanisms use a bang-bang controller variant with remote interfaces via RabbitMQ. Actuation ranges are governed by voltage settings (12V for baseline, 18V for enhanced uniformity). Safety constraints include a protocol in the low-level driver to prevent unsafe states. The controller regulates temperature by issuing commands to the fan and heatbed based on sensor data.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are temperature probes (T2, T3) with spatial distribution across the incubator. Sampling frequency and transmission rates are implied by real-time data upload to RabbitMQ. Accuracy is validated through calibration experiments (e.g., 6°C discrepancy in new setup vs. 7°C previously). Precision is ensured via parameter calibration (e.g., C_air and G_box values) and anomaly detection using Kalman filters.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity requires calibration experiments to refine parameters (e.g., Model B’s four parameters outperform two-parameter models for thermal inertia). Validation methods include simulation co-simulations with historical/real-time PT data and Kalman filter-based state estimation. Uncertainty sources are thermal inertia variations and sensor discrepancies (e.g., 6°C max difference). Error correction uses Kalman filters and self-adaptation loops to reconfigure controllers post-anomaly detection. Quality assurance involves open-source datasets and automated calibration processes.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature data from DS18S20 sensors (measured in °C with 0.5°C accuracy) is transmitted to RabbitMQ server via low-level driver. Data types include temperature readings (float32), timestamps (int64). Communication protocol: AMQP 0-9-1 (RabbitMQ). Transmission frequency: periodic readings (not specified exact rate) and event-triggered updates when controller commands change. Data quality assurance includes sensor calibration and filtering. Preprocessing steps involve normalization to 1-Wire bus protocol. Data integration mechanisms: RabbitMQ message queues for real-time streaming to DT for calibration (Model B parameters) and anomaly detection (Kalman filter).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands (fan speed, heatbed power) are sent via RabbitMQ to low-level driver. Command types: binary (on/off) for fan, analog (PWM) for heatbed. Message format: JSON with timestamp, command type, and value. Validation: safe protocol in low-level driver to prevent overcurrent (4A MOSFETs). Feedback systems: temperature sensor data feeds back to controller for PID-like bang-bang control. Conflict resolution: priority given to safety protocols over user commands. Command execution confirmation: status updates sent back through RabbitMQ.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication protocols: AMQP 0-9-1 (RabbitMQ), 1-Wire (DS18S20). Network topology: Edge (Raspberry Pi 4) -> Cloud/On-premise RabbitMQ server. Bandwidth: <100 Mbps (RabbitMQ lightweight). Latency: <100ms for local communication. Reliability: RabbitMQ message persistence and replication. Redundancy: Dual 1-Wire bus for sensors. Security: TLS encryption for RabbitMQ (if configured), 1-Wire authentication via 64-bit serial codes.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Hosting platform: Raspberry Pi 4 (edge) with RabbitMQ server (cloud/on-premise). Computational requirements: 1.5GHz quad-core CPU, 2GB RAM. Storage: Time-series database (not specified) for logged data. Scalability: Modular architecture allows adding sensors/actuators. Deployment models: Edge-first with cloud integration. Availability: 24/7 operation via Raspberry Pi's power supply and RabbitMQ's fault tolerance.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle includes development stages such as model calibration (Model A/B parameters: C_air=616.56, G_box=0.65 for Model A; C_air=486.12, G_box=0.856, C_heater=33.65, G_heater=0.87 for Model B), real-time visualization (dashboard via time series database), on-demand calibration (section 3.3), and self-adaptation loop (detects anomalies via Kalman filter, schedules experiments, reconfigures controller). Transition management involves updating models with calibration data and integrating new parameters. Governance includes open-source tooling (RabbitMQ, GitHub) and continuous model refinement.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Development uses RabbitMQ for communication (Raspberry Pi deployment), low-level drivers for sensor/actuator interaction, and Kalman filters for state estimation. Calibration experiments (section 3.3) generate data trajectories for model refinement. Evolution strategies include co-simulations for what-if scenarios, model parameter updates (e.g., Model B's heatbed inertia prediction), and iterative reconfiguration of controllers. Version control is implied through model iteration (Model A/B) and open-source repository management.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support includes anomaly detection via Kalman filters (Model B), real-time temperature visualization, and what-if simulations for controller optimization (e.g., fig. 8). Analytics involve differential equations for dynamic modeling and data correlation (e.g., heatbed temperature estimation). User interaction is via dashboards and automated reconfiguration. Confidence measures are derived from model calibration accuracy (Model B outperforms Model A in thermal inertia prediction).
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Complies with open-source standards (RabbitMQ [4], GitHub [6]), industry protocols (RabbitMQ as message broker), and CPS frameworks (cyber-physical integration). Interoperability is achieved through standardized communication (RabbitMQ), model parameterization (J K⁻¹ units), and open-access datasets. Certification requirements are not explicitly mentioned, but open-source compliance and protocol adherence (e.g., RabbitMQ) are emphasized.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware, software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology, we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware, software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology, we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).

employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.
